perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Except as set forth in Section 4.1(b) above, Inktomi shall supply to Microsoft written invoices for all amounts due under this Agreement, and payments will be due net [*] ([*]) days from Microsoft's receipt of such invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Except as set forth in Section 4.1(b) above, Inktomi shall supply to Microsoft written invoices for all amounts due under this Agreement, and payments will be due net 90 days from Microsoft's receipt of such invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to Net 90 could be problematic if it violates California Prompt Payment laws depending on the specific industry and nature of the services provided. Many smaller vendors cannot sustain cashflow with such long payment windows.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code 7159.5 regulates payment schedules for home improvement contracts, and mandates that payments must be tied to specific work progress, disallowing large upfront payments or extended payment timelines that are not linked to completed work. While this contract isn't a home improvement contract, it serves as an example of how California law regulates payment schedules to protect contractors and consumers. Setting a 'Net 90' payment term could be seen as an unreasonably long delay that puts undue financial strain on Inktomi, potentially violating the principle of fair and timely compensation.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the payment terms to 'Net 90 days,' which, according to the Perturbation Explanation, could violate California Prompt Payment laws, potentially causing cash flow problems for smaller vendors. The Law Explanation mentions California Business and Professions Code 7159.5, although related to home improvement contracts, as an example of regulating payment schedules to ensure fair compensation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 7159.5, explicitly applies to home improvement contracts, the principle it embodies—fair and timely payment—highlights the legal concern regarding unreasonably extended payment terms. The modified text may not directly violate BPC 7159.5, but the extended payment period directly contradicts the principle of timely compensation that Snippet 1 implies, creating a legal risk.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 Inktomi may terminate this Agreement without cause upon one year's prior written notice, provided that such notice may not be given prior to the second anniversary of the Launch Date.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 Inktomi may terminate this Agreement without cause upon 30 days' prior written notice, provided that such notice may not be given prior to the second anniversary of the Launch Date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 30 days could be deemed unconscionable or violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if Microsoft has made significant investments based on the original agreement's longer notice period. This is more likely if Inktomi is the dominant party.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2309(3) states that termination of a contract by one party requires reasonable notification and that an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Reducing the notice period to 30 days could be considered unconscionable if it deprives Microsoft of a reasonable opportunity to find a replacement hosting provider, particularly given the complexity and scale of the services provided. A year provides more reasonable time than just 30 days.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2309 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from one year to 30 days, which contradicts California Commercial Code § 2309(3), as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1. This snippet states that termination of a contract requires 'reasonable notification,' and an agreement dispensing with reasonable notification is invalid if unconscionable. While 'reasonable' isn't explicitly defined, the Law Explanation argues that 30 days is likely unreasonable given the context of the agreement, potentially depriving Microsoft of adequate time to find a replacement hosting provider, making the change unconscionable. This directly violates the condition in COM 2309(3), establishing a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.3 Inktomi shall keep true and accurate books and records, in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles ("GAAP"), relating to all costs and expenses for which Inktomi is entitled to charge Microsoft pursuant to Section 4.1 above, throughout the Term and for eighteen (18) additional months thereafter. Inktomi will permit Microsoft to have access to, and to make copies of, all such books and records for purposes of auditing and verifying such costs and expenses, provided that Microsoft shall give Inktomi reasonable notice prior to each requested audit and shall perform such audit during normal business hours at Inktomi's office(s) where such records are normally kept. If any Microsoft audit should determine that Inktomi overcharged Microsoft by an amount of [*]% or more for the period audited, then in addition to any and all other rights and remedies Microsoft may have under the circumstances, Microsoft may require Inktomi to reimburse it for all costs it incurred relating to such audit.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 Inktomi shall keep true and accurate books and records, in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles ("GAAP"), relating to all costs and expenses for which Inktomi is entitled to charge Microsoft pursuant to Section 4.1 above, throughout the Term and for six (6) additional months thereafter. Inktomi will permit Microsoft to have access to, and to make copies of, all such books and records for purposes of auditing and verifying such costs and expenses, provided that Microsoft shall give Inktomi one-week notice prior to each requested audit and shall perform such audit during normal business hours at Inktomi's office(s) where such records are normally kept. If any Microsoft audit should determine that Inktomi overcharged Microsoft by an amount of [*]% or more for the period audited, then in addition to any and all other rights and remedies Microsoft may have under the circumstances, Microsoft may require Inktomi to reimburse it for all costs it incurred relating to such audit.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record-keeping period to 6 months and requiring only one week's notice for audits may violate California's statute of limitations for breach of contract, which is four years for written contracts. This change limits Microsoft's ability to effectively pursue claims related to overcharging if discrepancies are discovered after the 6-month period or if they couldn't organize an audit within a week.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. By limiting the record-keeping period to six months, the contract effectively impairs Microsoft's ability to bring a claim for breach of contract related to overcharging beyond that period, as they would lack access to the necessary records. The shorter audit notice also inhibits their ability to effectively find any issues in a timely manner. This contractual limitation is less than the statutory period, creating a contradiction.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record-keeping period from eighteen months to six months and requires only one week's notice for audits. This directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 337, which establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 337 provides a four-year period for actions upon any contract. By limiting the record-keeping to six months, the contract impairs Microsoft's ability to bring a claim for breach of contract as they lack access to the necessary records. The one-week notice further inhibits their ability to find issues. Therefore, the changed text sets a requirement that violates state law, making the terms unenforceable.
